title: Why Everybody is WRONG about Oil Prices and the Iran War
transcript: Hey  guys,  Alex.  Hope  you're  well.  So,  in   this  video,  I  want  to  talk  to  you  about   something  many  have  been  asking  me   about,  which  is  the  price  of  crude  oil.   As  we've  seen,  crude  oil  prices  have   skyrocketed,  especially  in  the  wake  of   what's  happening  in  the  Middle  East  with   the  Iran  war.  And  obviously,  a  lot  of   people  are  quite  worried  and  they've   been  asking,  you  know,  what  could  happen   to  the  price  of  crude.  Are  we  going  to   be  in  the  middle  of  a  crisis  perhaps   even  of  gas  prices  likely  soaring?  And   what  are  the  charts  of  crude  oil  telling   us?  So,  we'll  have  a  look  at  these   important  charts.  All right,  guys.  Join   me.   All  right,  guys.  Welcome  back.  Speaking   today  from  Alan  Ana  in  Turkey.  Beautiful   place,  by  the  way.  All right,  guys.  So,   about  crude  oil.  Uh,  now  certainly  as   I'm  making  this  video  about  crude  oil,   as  I'm  sure  you  probably  know,  in  the   major  news  headlines,  we  have  the  Iran   war  and  of  course  news  about  the  straits   of  hormoons  in  the  Middle  East.  All   right,  guys.  So,  let's  talk  about  this.   First  of  all,  let  me  say  that  I  think   it's  a  dangerous  mistake  to  often   correlate  what's  happening  in  the  news   with  what's  happening  on  the  price   charts.  For  example,  the  price  of  crude   oil.  I  know  this  makes  common  sense  and   a  lot  of  people  unfortunately  still   think  that  prices  or  for  example  crude   oil  prices  or  price  of  anything  actually   in  the  markets  has  to  follow  some  kind   of  logical  common  sense.  That's  the   general  approach  that  a  lot  of  people   have  about  the  markets  and  unfortunately   it's  wrong.  It  is  incorrect.  In  fact,   there  have  been  several  studies  that   have  shown  that  news  has  very  little  to   do  with  what's  actually  going  on  on  the   price  charts  or  the  charts  of  any   market.  And  actually,  often  what  usually   happens  is  against  common  sense  and   against  logic.  So,  even  though  it's  very   easy  at  the  moment  to  look  at  the  chart   of  crude  oil  and  say,  "Ah,  okay,  the   price  is  skyrocketing  because  of  the   Iran  war."  And  certainly,  listen,  I'm   not  saying  that  uncertainties  and   worries  about  what's  happening  in  the   Middle  East  have  nothing  to  do  with  it.   No,  I'm  not  saying  that.  I  think  that   investors  in  general  are  worried  and   have  concerns  about  what's  happening  in   the  Middle  East  and  potentially  with   regard  to  price  of  crude  oil.  So  I  do   think  there's  a  general  fear  and  panic   which  is  behind  the  sentiment  with   regards  crude  oil.  And  as  we  know  price   is  driven  by  sentiment  by  fear,  greed,   optimism  and  pessimism,  not  so  much  the   news  itself.  And  that  is  why  the  price   of  a  market  or  commodity  often  has  very   little  to  do  what's  going  on  in  the   news.  Anyway,  what  I  want  to  focus  on  is   looking  at  the  chart  of  crude  oil  from  a   technical  perspective,  not  a   fundamentals  perspective.  Because  as  we   know,  fundamentals  lag  price.  We  have  to   remember  that  doing  analysis  by  looking   at  fundamentals  is  often  like  driving   but  only  looking  at  the  rearview  mirror.   I  really  think  that  it's  important  to   look  at  what  the  charts  are  telling  us.   And  as  you  can  see  here,  guys,  the  chart   of  crude  oil  as  it  skyrocketed  and  went   over  $100  a  barrel  extremely  fast.  As  we   can  see  here,  crude  oil  broke  out  of   those  trend  lines  that  we  have  there,   the  channel,  and  went  very  quickly  to   about  $100  and  actually  went  over  $100  a   barrel.  And  here's  the  thing,  guys.   Because  crude  oil  prices  became   parabolic,  they  also  went  too  far  too   fast.  And  what's  happened  is,  as  you  can   see  on  the  chart,  crude  oil  has  now   triggered  what's  called  an  acceleration   extreme.  And  not  just  any  acceleration   extreme,  but  red  and  purple  acceleration   extremes.  And  actually,  we  changed  the   settings  to  strict.  You  can  see  that  on   the  strict  setting  of  the  acceleration   extreme  indicator,  we  have  a  red   acceleration  extreme.  So  again,  this   shows  extremely  overstretched  overbought   conditions  on  the  price  of  crude  oil.   And  here's  the  thing,  guys.  You're   probably  wondering,  well,  okay,  so  what?   Well,  here's  the  thing,  guys.  If  you   look  at  previous  times  when  crude  oil   has  triggered  these  extreme   accelerations,  okay,  these  extreme   overbought,  overstretched  conditions,   you  will  see  what  crude  oil  has  done   afterwards.  For  example,  the  last  time   this  triggered  was  in  2022.  In  the   spring  of  2022,  we  had  the  price  of   crude  oil  skyrocketing,  went  up  very   fast,  as  you  can  see  here.  But  look  at   this.  We  had  extreme  acceleration.  We   had  red  and  purple  acceleration   extremes.  And  notice  what  happened.  Then   the  price  of  crude  oil  pretty  much  made   a  top  and  then  dropped  quite  sharply.  It   actually  had  a  major  pullback  and   actually  over  the  course  of  time,  it   then  gradually  declined  further.  So  it   did  not  actually  go  much  higher  than   that.  and  as  a  matter  of  fact,  form  the   top  right  there.  Here's  another  example   for  you  from  the  year  1996.  Again,  the   price  of  crude  oil  rallied  strongly.  We   had  red  and  purple  acceleration  extremes   again  showing  extreme  overbought   conditions.  And  look  at  this.  Then  the   price  of  crude  oil  pretty  much  made  the   top  and  then  dropped  quite  sharply.  As   as  a  matter  of  fact,  it  dropped  back  to   the  averages  and  declined  in  doubledigit   percentages.  All right,  guys.  So,  a  very   swift  and  very  sharp  drop  in  the  price   of  crude.  The  one  before  that,  by  the   way,  you  can  see  a  couple  of  examples.   in  the  year  1990.  So  you  can  see  in  the   year  1990  the  first  time  that  occurred   again  exploration  extremes  occurred  on   the  price  of  crude  oil  and  then  pretty   much  oil  then  topped  there  and  then   dropped  quite  sharply  back  to  its   averages.  There  are  some  other  examples   in  the  year  1990.  I  want  to  show  you   here.  Now  the  first  couple  of  times  when   the  acceleration  extreme  occurred,  oil   made  a  slight  pullback  but  then  continue   to  rally  further  higher.  But  look  at   this.  The  next  time  that  the  price  of   crude  went  up  and  then  triggered  an   acceleration  extreme,  you  can  see  it   formed  a  top,  okay?  And  then  pretty  much   it  then  dropped  quite  sharply  back  down   to  its  averages.  So  guys,  looking  at   these  examples,  we  can  see  that  in   previous  times  in  history  when  the  price   of  crude  oil  has  skyrocketed,  have   become  parabolic  and  triggered   acceleration  extremes  again  2022,  1996   and  1990  several  times.  We  can  see  a   general  pattern  that  the  price  of  crude   oil  pretty  much  soon  afterwards  makes  a   top.  In  other  words,  becomes  extremely   overstretched,  overbought,  and  then  it   drops  back  down  usually  in  double  digit   percentages  back  to  its  averages  and   sometimes  it  forms  a  long-term  top  as   well.  Now,  we  can  see  right  now  we  have   very  similar  situation.  Now,  I  know  some   people  are  going  to  say,  well,  this  time   is  different  because  of  the  Iran  war  and   things  like  that.  Guys,  the  fact  is  in   previous  occasions  too,  people  have   always  said  this  time  is  different  and   it  wasn't.  So  we  have  to  be  very   cautious  when  people  say  oh  this  time  is   different  because  of  so  and  so  reasons.   No,  we  need  to  remain  as  objective  as   possible  given  the  extreme  overbought   conditions,  overstretch  conditions  with   acceleration  extremes  as  we  can  see  here   right  now  in  2026  as  I'm  making  this  in   March  of  2026  with  oil  also  reaching   resistance  and  again  becoming  extremely   overstretched  overbought  parabolic  with   extreme  acceleration  readings.  I  think   the  probabilities  are  quite  high  that  we   could  see  some  kind  of a  top  forming  in   the  next  several  weeks  and  months.  Now   again  this  doesn't  have  to  happen   immediately.  It  is  possible  for  bulls  to   push  this  a  bit  further  higher  but  I   think  we  need  to  watch  the  price  of   crude  oil  very  carefully.  If  oil  can   break  down  below  this  support  at  76.73   it  would  significantly  increase  the   probability  of  a  top  in  the  price  of   crude  oil  and  then  drop  back  to  its   averages.  Now  this  has  not  happened  as   yet  but  I  think  it  could  potentially   happen  in  the  next  several  weeks  and   months.  And  by  the  way  this  video  is  by   no  means  a  recommendation  to  sell  or   short  or  buy  into  crude  oil.  This  video   is  purely  for  educational  purposes.  So   all  I  want  to  do  is  shed  some  light   what's  going  on  on  the  chart  of  crude   oil.  And  to  me  uh  based  on  what  I'm   seeing  here,  I  think  this  particular   rally  has  run  its  course.  I  think  there   are  strong  indications  here  on  oil  that   we  could  be  reaching  a  likely  top  in  the   next  several  weeks  and  months.  Even  if   it  continues  to  push  a  bit  further   higher,  I  think  this  rally  is  going  to   be  temporary.  And  guys,  about  the  Iran   war,  I  think  that  also  is  more  likely  to   be  temporary  as  well.  I  don't  think  the   situation  with  the  Iran  war  is  going  to   continue  for  a  very  long  time.  I  think   it's  going to  be  temporary.  I  think  the   price  of  crude  oil  is  likely  to  top  in   the  next  several  weeks  and  months  before   any  major  news  comes  out  about  Iran   before  some  potential  positive  news   comes  out  because  as  we  know  price  often   moves  ahead  of  the  news  again   fundamentals  are  usually  lagging  and  by   the  way  guys  there's  one  other  thing   which  makes  me  cautious  which  is  that   XLE  the  energy  or  oil  stocks  ETF  this   chart  has  not  confirmed  the  recent  rally   we've  seen  the  price  of  crude  oil  so   whereas  oil  has  continued  to  skyrocket   higher  XLE  as  a  matter  of  fact  has  not   confirmed  that  it  has  pulled  back  and   dropped.  So  that  non-confirmation  on  the   chart  of  XLE  should  make  us  cautious   here.  So  in  a  nutshell,  guys,  uh  I  think   that  in  the  next  several  months,  uh   we're  going  to  see  the  price  of  crude   oil  eventually  dropping  back  down  below   80  and  likely  dropping  back  down  to  its   averages.  And  as  mentioned,  oil  has  to   break  down  below  this  recent  low,  this   recent  support  at  76.73  to  increase  the   probability  that  a  top  has  formed.  So  I   don't  think  this  particular  rally  is   going  to  continue  for  too  long.  I  think   it's  a  short-term  rally  and  I  think  in   the  next  several  weeks  and  months  we're   going  to  see  a  top  potentially  a   long-term  top  or  a  medium-term  top  in   the  price  of  crude  which  means  that  even   if  fuel  prices  or  gas  prices  go  up  in   the  short  term  I  don't  think  it's  going   to  last  too  long.  I  think  fuel  prices   and  gas  prices  eventually  will  drop  back   down  by  the  end  of  this  year  to  lower   prices.  Once  crude  oil  breaks  support,   then  the  odds  increase  for  some  kind  of   pullback  correction  and  indeed  a  major   drop  down.  All right,  guys.  Hope  this   video  helps.  If  it  has,  please  give  it  a   like  and  subscribe  and  I'll  see  you  guys   next  video  up.  Cheers.
summary_hu: A videó elemzi a nyersolaj árának gyors emelkedését, amely a Közel-Keleten zajló Irán háborújával párhuzamosan történt. A narrátor hangsúlyozza, hogy a hírek és az árak közötti korreláció gyakran félrevezető, mivel a piaci érzések és a befektetők aggodalmai sokkal inkább befolyásolják az árakat. Emeli, hogy a tankönyvi megközelítés helyett fontosabb a műszaki elemzésre koncentrálni, a múlt során tapasztalt hasonlóságok alapján. Korábbi eseteket vizsgálva példákat hoz fel, ahol a nyersolaj árának parabolikus emelkedése után jelentős visszaesés következett. Ez alapján valószínűsíti, hogy a közeljövőben olajár-csúcs alakulhat ki, amely után a piaci árak jelentősen csökkenhetnek. A narrátor végül óvatosságot javasol a jelenlegi piaci helyzet ismeretében.
summary_en: The analysis focuses on the recent surge in crude oil prices, which coincides with the ongoing Iran war in the Middle East. The narrator emphasizes that correlating news events with price movements can be misleading, as market sentiments and investors' fears often drive prices. He advocates for a technical analysis approach over fundamental perspectives, citing historical instances where parabolic price increases were followed by significant declines. By examining past examples, he suggests a potential peak in oil prices could occur in the near term, followed by a sharp decrease. Ultimately, he advises caution given the current overbought conditions in the market.
key_points_hu: A hírek és az árak közötti korreláció gyakran félrevezető.
A műszaki elemzés fontosabb a fundamentális elemzésnél, különösen az olajpiacon.
A múlt tapasztalatai alapján a nyersolaj árának parabolikus emelkedése után várhatóan csökkenés következik.
key_points_en: There's often a misleading correlation between news events and price movements.
Technical analysis is more important than fundamental analysis, especially in the oil market.
Based on historical patterns, a parabolic rise in oil prices is likely to lead to a subsequent decline.
